client.name: Yara International ASA
client.tagline: Responsibly feed the world and protect the planet
client.subtitle: Agriculture & Chemicals | Crop Nutrition, Ammonia & Industrial Solutions
client.description: Yara International ASA is a Norwegian chemical company and global leader in crop nutrition, producing and distributing nitrogen-based fertilizers, ammonia, and industrial chemicals to over 160 countries. Founded in 1905 and headquartered in Oslo, Norway, Yara serves the farming community, food value chain, and clean energy transition industries — combining a core fertilizer production business with a fast-growing digital farming platform. With $15.7 billion in annual revenues and 23.76 million tonnes of fertilizer delivered annually, Yara operates at the convergence of agricultural science, industrial chemistry, and digital innovation.

keyFacts.label: Founded
keyFacts.desc: Established as part of Norsk Hydro's electrochemical operations; incorporated as independent Yara International ASA in 2004 and listed on the Oslo Stock Exchange (OSE: YAR)

keyFacts.label: Employees
keyFacts.desc: Global workforce spanning production plants, agronomy, digital engineering, commercial, and corporate functions across 60+ countries

keyFacts.label: Revenue
keyFacts.desc: Full year 2025 revenues diversified across Crop Nutrition, Industrial Solutions, and Yara Clean Ammonia segments

keyFacts.label: Fertilizer Deliveries
keyFacts.desc: Annual crop nutrition product deliveries globally as reported in the 2025 Annual Report

serviceAreas.title: Crop Nutrition
serviceAreas.desc: Develops, produces, and markets the full spectrum of nitrogen, phosphate, and potassium fertilizers and agronomic solutions under global brands including YaraBela, YaraMila, YaraLiva, and YaraVita.

serviceAreas.title: Industrial Solutions
serviceAreas.desc: Supplies nitrogen-based industrial chemicals — including nitric acid, technical ammonium nitrate, AdBlue/DEF, and CO₂ — to mining, automotive, food processing, and environmental sectors worldwide.

serviceAreas.title: Yara Clean Ammonia
serviceAreas.desc: Positions Yara as an enabler of the hydrogen economy by producing, transporting, and marketing low-carbon and renewable ammonia as a shipping fuel and industrial energy carrier for decarbonisation.

serviceAreas.title: Digital Farming
serviceAreas.desc: Develops AI-powered precision agriculture platforms — including the Atfarm application and satellite-based crop monitoring services — that translate agronomic science into data-driven recommendations for farmers globally.

priorities.title: Digital Farming Platform Scalability and Innovation
priorities.desc: Yara's Atfarm platform is scaling to millions of farmers globally, requiring engineering teams to build resilient, low-latency APIs and ML-powered crop recommendation engines. Teams must handle real-time satellite imagery ingestion, multi-tenant SaaS architecture, and integration of agronomic science models at speed across 60+ markets. Engineering velocity on Atfarm directly translates to farmer outcomes and commercial differentiation in precision agriculture.
priorities.roles: Software Engineers, QA Engineers, Tech Leads & Architects
priorities.pains: Complex domain logic in agronomic models, multi-language localisation across global markets, high data volumes from satellite and IoT sources, tight integration with SAP and third-party farm management systems

priorities.title: Industrial and Production System Integration Modernisation
priorities.tagline: Bridging OT and IT at scale
priorities.desc: Yara's software engineers maintain and extend systems bridging operational technology on production plants with enterprise IT, including SAP S/4HANA integrations, SCADA interfaces, and supply chain automation. This requires deep understanding of nitrogen production processes, safety-critical system constraints, and zero-downtime deployment practices. Modernisation of legacy plant control integrations is a continuous priority competing for the same engineering capacity as digital farming product delivery.
priorities.roles: Software Engineers, DevOps & Platform, Security Engineers
priorities.pains: Legacy SCADA and historian systems with limited API surface, strict change control in OT environments, incompatible security posture between plant networks and cloud, long lead times driven by Change Advisory Board governance

priorities.title: Cloud-Native Architecture and Azure Platform Adoption
priorities.desc: Yara is accelerating migration of legacy on-premise workloads to Microsoft Azure, standardising on Kubernetes, event-driven microservices, and Azure Data Factory for agricultural data pipelines. Platform engineering teams are building an internal developer platform to reduce onboarding friction and standardise deployment patterns across squads. The goal is a unified technology estate that enables rapid product iteration across both digital farming and industrial domains.
priorities.roles: DevOps & Platform, Tech Leads & Architects, Software Engineers
priorities.pains: Heterogeneous legacy estate with mixed technology stacks, SAP and Azure integration complexity, cloud cost governance as Atfarm user base scales, skill gaps in cloud-native patterns across globally distributed engineering teams

priorities.title: Decarbonisation and Sustainability Technology Delivery
priorities.tagline: Software for a greener food system
priorities.desc: Yara's Climate Choice programme and Carbon Capture and Storage project require engineering teams to build carbon footprint calculators, emissions tracking pipelines, and regulatory reporting systems to investor-grade accuracy standards. Engineers must collaborate with sustainability scientists to model fertilizer carbon intensity, integrate third-party emissions data, and surface insights to farmers and enterprise customers. Accuracy, auditability, and regulatory compliance are non-negotiable engineering requirements.
priorities.roles: Software Engineers, Product & Planning, QA Engineers
priorities.pains: Rapidly evolving regulatory frameworks (EU CSRD, SFDR, national equivalents), complex emissions calculation methodologies, integration with diverse supply chain data sources, mandatory explainability and auditability for calculation outputs

priorities.title: Agricultural Data Platform and AI-Driven Agronomic Intelligence
priorities.tagline: Turning field data into farming decisions
priorities.desc: Yara is building a unified agricultural data platform consolidating satellite imagery, soil sensor data, weather feeds, and farm management records to power AI-driven crop recommendations at scale. Data engineering teams create gold-standard datasets for training agronomic ML models, while software engineers expose these capabilities through APIs consumed by Atfarm and partner platforms. Data governance, quality, model explainability, and farmer data sovereignty are the central engineering constraints.
priorities.roles: Software Engineers, Tech Leads & Architects, DevOps & Platform
priorities.pains: Inconsistent data formats across markets and data partners, high storage and compute costs for satellite imagery processing, model drift in seasonal agronomic predictions, regulatory constraints on farmer data sharing across jurisdictions

capabilities.objectives: Yara's software engineers design and build the digital farming platforms, industrial integration middleware, and data pipelines that underpin both commercial and sustainability ambitions. They work across the Atfarm product family, SAP integration layers, and cloud-native microservices deployed on Azure Kubernetes Service, operating in cross-functional squads aligned to domains such as Precision Agronomy, Clean Ammonia Operations, and Industrial Supply Chain. Engineers must bridge software craftsmanship with deep agricultural science context.
capabilities.painPoints: Navigating complex agronomic domain logic with limited structured documentation, context-switching between Python data services and TypeScript front-ends, debugging intricate SAP BAPI and RFC integrations that require specialist knowledge, collaborating across Oslo, Tampa, Singapore, and farm-facing market hubs with narrow synchronous windows
capabilities.keyActivities: Feature development in Atfarm, SAP integration layer maintenance, satellite imagery pipeline engineering, REST and GraphQL API design, code review in Azure DevOps, sprint ceremonies, agronomic model integration, microservice development in Python and TypeScript

capabilities.objectives: QA Engineers at Yara validate that digital farming software meets both functional and agronomic accuracy standards, ensuring crop recommendations, emissions calculations, and farm data workflows behave correctly across a vast diversity of crops, geographies, and growing conditions. They own test strategy for the Atfarm platform and industrial integration services, maintaining automated regression suites and coordinating user acceptance testing with agronomy scientists and commercial market leads.
capabilities.painPoints: Writing test cases covering complex multi-variable agronomic logic where correctness requires crop science expertise, reproducing environment-specific bugs tied to regional crop seasons and soil types, maintaining test datasets representative of 60+ global market conditions, limited environments that mirror production SAP configurations, balancing automated coverage with exploratory testing for new ML recommendation features
capabilities.keyActivities: Automated regression testing for Atfarm APIs, performance testing of satellite data ingestion pipelines, UAT coordination with agronomy and commercial stakeholders, test environment management in Azure, defect triage in Jira, integration test suites for SAP connectors, agronomic calculation validation

capabilities.objectives: Tech Leads and Architects at Yara set technical direction for digital farming and industrial software, ensuring that architectural decisions align with Yara's Azure-first cloud strategy, SAP landscape, and long-term agricultural data platform goals. They own domain architecture for the Atfarm product family, define API contracts and data models shared across squads, and guide teams through complex integrations with legacy production systems, OT environments, and external agricultural data partners.
capabilities.painPoints: Balancing short-term squad velocity with long-term architectural coherence across a globally distributed engineering organisation, managing SAP S/4HANA as both a data source and transaction system within modern cloud patterns, aligning OT integration requirements with cloud-native architectural principles, creating architectural documentation accessible to engineers across varied geographies and domain experience levels
capabilities.keyActivities: Architecture Decision Records (ADRs), agricultural domain data modelling, API contract governance, cross-squad technical alignment sessions, Azure Well-Architected Framework reviews, SAP landscape planning, engineering guild facilitation, technical roadmap contribution, OT-IT integration pattern design

capabilities.objectives: Product and Planning teams translate commercial priorities and agronomic insights into engineering roadmaps for digital farming and industrial software. They collaborate with agronomy scientists, crop nutrition commercial leads, and engineering squads to define features for Atfarm, emissions tracking tools, and digital supply chain services. They own the product backlog, manage release planning across multiple markets with distinct regulatory and localisation requirements, and track adoption metrics across Yara's global farmer base.
capabilities.painPoints: Translating nuanced agronomic requirements into well-specified engineering tasks without crop science expertise, managing roadmap conflicts between farmer-facing feature requests and platform modernisation work, coordinating releases across 60+ market localisation requirements, gathering qualitative feedback from farmers in developing markets with limited digital connectivity, aligning sustainability reporting timelines with product feature delivery cycles
capabilities.keyActivities: Product backlog grooming in Jira, OKR setting and tracking, stakeholder alignment across commercial, agronomy, and IT, user story writing for agronomic features, release planning in Azure DevOps, market prioritisation workshops, adoption analytics in Power BI, farmer journey mapping

capabilities.objectives: DevOps and Platform engineers build and operate the internal developer platform on Microsoft Azure that enables software engineering squads to deliver digital farming and industrial software reliably and at speed. They own CI/CD pipelines in Azure DevOps, manage AKS clusters hosting Atfarm microservices, and operate data infrastructure including Azure Data Factory, Azure Databricks, and Cosmos DB. Platform engineering also owns the observability stack, manages cloud cost governance, and maintains on-call coverage for Atfarm production incidents.
capabilities.painPoints: Managing complex multi-environment deployment pipelines spanning digital farming, industrial, and corporate IT workloads on different change cadences, enforcing cost controls as Azure spend scales with Atfarm user growth, maintaining high availability SLAs for APIs consumed by farm advisors and precision agriculture equipment, integrating legacy SCADA and historian systems with cloud-native CI/CD standards, providing incident response coverage across dispersed global time zones
capabilities.keyActivities: Azure Kubernetes Service operations and capacity management, CI/CD pipeline development in Azure DevOps, Azure Data Factory pipeline maintenance, infrastructure as code with Terraform, observability with Azure Monitor and Application Insights, cloud cost governance dashboards, on-call incident response, platform engineering roadmap

capabilities.objectives: Security Engineers protect a uniquely complex attack surface spanning cloud-hosted digital farming platforms, SAP financial and supply chain systems, OT networks in nitrogen and ammonia production facilities, and farmer agronomic data held across 60+ jurisdictions. They develop and enforce security standards for Atfarm and industrial integrations, conduct threat modelling for new features, and oversee compliance with GDPR and emerging agricultural data regulations. The intersection of critical national infrastructure (nitrogen plants) and consumer SaaS (Atfarm) creates a security posture challenge found in few other engineering organisations.
capabilities.painPoints: Securing the IT-OT boundary at production plants where nitrogen and ammonia processing is classified as critical infrastructure in multiple jurisdictions, managing identity and access across SAP, Azure AD, and partner farm management systems, ensuring GDPR-compliant handling of farmer agronomic data across jurisdictions with divergent regulatory interpretations, keeping pace with cloud-native and AI feature development velocity while maintaining security review rigour
capabilities.keyActivities: Threat modelling for Atfarm features, penetration testing coordination, IT/OT security boundary design and review, Azure Security Center monitoring, GDPR data mapping and DPIAs, SAP security role review, security code review in pull request process, zero trust architecture implementation, incident response playbook maintenance

workflows.name: Digital Farming Feature Development

steps.d: Product Manager collaborates with agronomy scientists and commercial market leads to define feature requirements; agronomic accuracy specifications are documented alongside user stories in Jira; crop science constraints translated into engineering acceptance criteria

Tech Lead creates an Architecture Decision Record and API contract; squad reviews the design in a technical alignment session; dependencies on SAP, external satellite data providers, or partner farm management systems are identified and scoped

Software Engineers implement features in two-week sprints; front-end TypeScript and back-end Python services developed in parallel; agronomic model integration handled by data scientists embedded in the squad; pull requests opened in Azure DevOps

Pull requests reviewed by peers and Tech Lead against agronomic logic correctness and platform standards; QA Engineers execute automated regression suites and exploratory tests across representative crop, soil, and market scenario combinations

steps.t: UAT with Agronomy and Market Teams
steps.d: Agronomy team validates crop recommendation accuracy against field trial datasets; commercial market leads conduct localisation review for target geographies; sign-off required from agronomy before release approval is granted

DevOps Engineer deploys to AKS via Azure DevOps pipeline using a phased market rollout; release notes published to internal stakeholders; adoption metrics monitored in Power BI; post-release agronomic telemetry reviewed by data science team

workflows.friction: Agronomic validation with domain experts is the most consistent delivery bottleneck — UAT cycles are slow because agronomy scientists operate on crop-season schedules, their feedback is qualitative, and engineers lack the vocabulary to resolve ambiguities without prolonged back-and-forth cycles that can extend a sprint by days

workflows.name: Production Plant Integration Release

steps.d: Software Engineer or plant operations team raises a formal change request for any modification to SAP-OT integration, historian data pipeline, or SCADA interface; a standardised risk assessment form is completed and submitted to the Change Advisory Board queue

Security Engineer and OT network team jointly assess change impact on the plant network boundary; any modification to safety-critical control systems requires written sign-off from the plant safety officer before development may proceed

Engineers develop and test changes in a sandboxed environment configured to mirror production SAP; full OT integration testing requires access to a physical plant test environment, which must be scheduled and approved separately

CAB reviews the submitted risk assessment, test evidence, and rollback plan; high-risk industrial integration changes go through an extended CAB process involving plant operations, IT security, and legal; approval can take two to four weeks

DevOps deploys changes during a pre-approved plant maintenance window aligned with production shutdowns; zero-downtime deployment is not always achievable in OT contexts; deployment is accompanied by plant engineer standby

Platform team monitors historian data flows and SAP transaction throughput for 72 hours; plant operators validate operational data continuity; formal change closure completed in the ITSM system after hyper-care period

workflows.friction: The CAB approval process and mandatory maintenance window scheduling create lead times of weeks to months for even minor industrial integration changes, generating a stark velocity gap between Atfarm digital product delivery and plant IT modernisation and frustrating engineers accustomed to continuous delivery practices

workflows.name: Atfarm Platform Incident Response

steps.d: Azure Monitor or Application Insights generates an automated alert; PagerDuty notifies the on-call DevOps Engineer; initial triage begins within five minutes for P1 incidents affecting farmer-facing services

On-call engineer assesses severity using Yara's incident classification matrix; P1 and P2 incidents trigger a dedicated Microsoft Teams war room channel; an incident commander is assigned from the senior on-call rotation

Engineers query Application Insights distributed traces, Cosmos DB metrics, and AKS pod logs; the microservice dependency graph is consulted to isolate root cause; satellite data pipeline health is checked separately when agronomic API responses are degraded

Incident commander approves mitigation action — options include Azure DevOps pipeline rollback, feature flag toggle, AKS pod restart, or traffic rerouting via Azure Front Door; Product Manager and affected market leads are notified if farmer-facing functionality is impaired

Service restoration confirmed by monitoring recovery metrics; Atfarm status page updated; affected regional commercial leads receive an incident summary; full incident timeline documented in Confluence

Post-mortem conducted within 48 hours of P1 resolution; root cause, contributing factors, and action items documented in Confluence using the standard Yara post-mortem template; action items tracked as Jira tickets assigned to responsible squads

workflows.friction: Diagnosis is significantly slowed when agronomic data pipelines are involved — engineers need simultaneous knowledge of Azure infrastructure behaviour and the agricultural data models to distinguish a platform bug from a data quality issue from a seasonal agronomic model anomaly, and that intersection of skills is rare in any single engineer

workflows.name: New Engineer Onboarding

steps.d: IT provisions Azure AD account, Azure DevOps and Jira/Confluence access, SAP read-only training credentials, and Microsoft 365 E5 licence; hardware shipped to home office or collected from regional hub; PagerDuty on-call profile created

New engineer completes mandatory Yara global induction covering mission and values, safety culture and TRI expectations, sustainability commitments, and Code of Conduct; HR onboarding completed through Workday including benefits and compliance training

Squad Tech Lead walks engineer through local development environment setup; Atfarm codebase cloned from Azure DevOps; dev containers configured using squad-standard tooling; access to Azure development subscription granted with appropriate RBAC roles

Engineer attends a structured three-session agronomic onboarding with Yara agronomists covering crop nutrition fundamentals, the NPK product portfolio, and the farm advisory context that shapes product requirements; there is no formal written learning path for this domain knowledge

Engineer picks up a well-scoped starter ticket in Jira labelled for onboarding; completes first pull request reviewed by a squad peer and Tech Lead; deploys to the development environment through the Azure DevOps CI/CD pipeline to validate setup end-to-end

Engineer joins all sprint ceremonies, attends the cross-squad architecture guild, and is assigned a peer buddy for the first 90 days; engineering manager sets 30-60-90 day goals aligned to squad OKRs; first performance check-in scheduled in Workday

workflows.friction: The agronomic domain immersion is the most significant and least resolved onboarding bottleneck — engineers join Yara with strong software skills but zero crop nutrition knowledge, and the three informal sessions with agronomists are insufficient to write meaningful, correct code for Atfarm without months of gradual accretion through trial and error

individual.name: Agronomic Domain Knowledge Gap
individual.desc: Software engineers must write code modelling complex crop nutrition science — nitrogen uptake curves, soil pH interactions, multi-nutrient balance recommendations — but receive no structured technical learning path. This leads to frequent clarification loops with agronomists and risk of silent logic errors in crop recommendation features that only surface during UAT or, worse, in production.

individual.name: SAP Integration Debugging Complexity
individual.desc: Yara's SAP S/4HANA landscape is the backbone of supply chain and finance, but debugging BAPI and RFC integration failures requires specialist knowledge that few software engineers possess. This creates long diagnosis cycles and heavy reliance on a small number of SAP-specialist colleagues who become bottlenecks across multiple squads.

individual.name: Satellite and Geospatial Data Handling
individual.desc: Engineers working on Atfarm's satellite imagery features must handle large geospatial datasets — GeoTIFF rasters, shapefiles, NDVI and LAI indices — using specialist libraries and cloud compute configurations outside most software engineers' standard background. Onboarding to geospatial tooling is largely self-directed with limited internal documentation.

individual.name: Multi-Timezone Collaboration Overhead
individual.desc: Yara's engineering squads span Oslo, Tampa, Singapore, and other hubs, meaning synchronous collaboration windows are narrow and asynchronous communication demands high-quality written specifications. Code review feedback, architectural decisions, and dependency negotiations carry multi-hour latency that compounds across a sprint.

individual.name: Legacy Codebase Navigation
individual.desc: Portions of the Atfarm platform and industrial integration services carry code from earlier digital farming experiments with minimal documentation, making it slow for engineers to understand original intent before safely modifying behaviour or refactoring components toward cloud-native standards.

individual.name: Slow Integration Test Feedback Loops
individual.desc: Automated test suites for Atfarm — particularly suites validating agronomic calculation correctness across thousands of crop-market-soil combinations — take significant time to execute, slowing developer feedback loops, discouraging local test runs, and reducing iteration speed within a sprint.

individual.name: Regulatory Variance Across Operating Markets
individual.desc: Fertilizer application regulations, farmer data privacy laws (GDPR, national equivalents), and agronomic reporting standards vary substantially across Yara's 60+ markets, creating a complex localisation and compliance landscape that engineers must navigate when building or modifying market-facing features.

team.name: Engineering–Agronomy Communication Gap
team.desc: Software engineering squads and the agronomy science team speak fundamentally different professional languages, creating friction throughout the delivery lifecycle from requirements definition to defect reporting. Misaligned terminology causes features that are technically correct to be agronomically inaccurate, discovered only late in UAT cycles.

team.name: Cross-Squad Dependency Management
team.desc: Atfarm's microservice architecture means most significant features span multiple squads, requiring coordination on API contracts, shared data model changes, and release sequencing. Cross-squad dependencies are a leading cause of sprint scope changes and are managed through ad hoc Jira linking rather than a systematic dependency tracking process.

team.name: Test Environment Scarcity
team.desc: Full integration testing against realistic SAP and OT system configurations requires dedicated test environments that are expensive to provision and maintain in Azure, creating queuing and scheduling conflicts across squads that need parallel test slots during the final days of a sprint.

team.name: Knowledge Concentration Risk
team.desc: Deep knowledge of critical system components — including SAP integration middleware, agronomic model implementations, satellite pipeline architecture, and OT connectivity layers — is concentrated in a small number of individuals, creating delivery risk and bottlenecks whenever those engineers are unavailable, on leave, or transition out of their roles.

team.name: Documentation Debt
team.desc: Fast-moving product development has consistently outpaced documentation effort, leaving architectural decisions, agronomic model logic, and integration patterns poorly documented in Confluence. New squad members and cross-squad collaborators regularly rediscover information that already exists in someone's head or an out-of-date wiki page.

organisation.name: Dual-Speed IT: Digital Product vs. OT Change Cadence
organisation.desc: Yara operates two fundamentally incompatible IT regimes — a fast-moving Azure-native digital farming product organisation and a highly regulated, safety-critical OT environment at production plants with multi-week change cycles. These regimes compete for engineering resource, create organisational friction, and make it difficult to apply consistent engineering standards across the enterprise.

organisation.name: Global Engineering Talent Competition
organisation.desc: As Yara competes for software engineering talent in Oslo, Amsterdam, and other European tech hubs, it faces direct competition from pure-play technology companies offering higher base compensation and more conventional engineering roles. Retaining engineers who develop rare combinations of software skill and Yara domain expertise is a persistent strategic risk.

organisation.name: Sustainability Reporting Accuracy and Auditability
organisation.desc: Regulatory frameworks including EU CSRD, SFDR, and national equivalents mandate investor-grade accuracy in emissions and sustainability disclosures. Engineering teams must build and maintain carbon calculation pipelines where methodology errors carry significant legal and reputational consequences, adding a compliance dimension to standard product delivery that requires specialist engagement.

organisation.name: Agricultural Data Governance Across Jurisdictions
organisation.desc: Farmer agronomic data collected through Atfarm and partner integrations is subject to evolving data sovereignty regulations and farmer trust expectations across 60+ markets with differing legal frameworks. Building consistent data governance that satisfies local regulations while enabling global AI model training and cross-market analytics is an unresolved architectural and policy challenge.

organisation.name: Legacy Modernisation Backlog vs. Innovation Capacity
organisation.desc: The volume of legacy industrial integration code, SAP customisations, and early-generation digital farming components requiring cloud-native refactoring substantially exceeds available engineering capacity, creating a persistent technical debt backlog that directly constrains the bandwidth available for new product innovation and platform capability investment.

technology.m365Status: Yara is a Microsoft strategic partner with Microsoft 365 E5 deployed across the global workforce, with Microsoft Teams as the primary platform for collaboration, incident response war rooms, and cross-geography squad coordination. Power BI is embedded in commercial and operational reporting workflows, and GitHub Copilot has been piloted in select engineering squads as part of Yara's broader AI productivity programme ahead of a wider Claude Code rollout.

platforms.name: Atfarm
platforms.desc: Yara's flagship digital farming SaaS platform delivering satellite-based field monitoring, nitrogen application planning, and agronomic recommendations to farmers and advisors globally; the primary software product built and maintained by Yara's digital engineering squads

platforms.name: SAP S/4HANA
platforms.desc: Core ERP system underpinning Yara's supply chain, finance, procurement, and production planning; integrated with Atfarm and digital farming APIs for order-to-cash, demand planning, and fertilizer delivery tracking workflows

platforms.name: Microsoft Azure
platforms.desc: Primary cloud infrastructure platform hosting Atfarm microservices on Azure Kubernetes Service, data pipelines via Azure Data Factory and Databricks, and persistence in Azure Blob Storage, Cosmos DB, and Azure SQL

platforms.name: Azure DevOps
platforms.desc: Source control, CI/CD pipeline orchestration, and work item tracking platform used across Yara's software engineering squads for Atfarm product development and digital platform delivery

platforms.name: Azure Databricks
platforms.desc: Distributed data processing and ML platform used by Yara's data engineering and data science teams for agronomic model training, satellite imagery batch processing, and soil analytics at scale

platforms.name: Azure Data Factory
platforms.desc: ETL and data integration service ingesting satellite imagery, weather data, soil sensor feeds, and SAP supply chain data into Yara's unified agricultural data platform for Atfarm consumption

platforms.name: Cropsat
platforms.desc: Yara's satellite crop monitoring service providing NDVI, NDRE, and LAI vegetation indices from Sentinel-2 and commercial satellites; powers the field mapping and variable-rate application features within Atfarm

platforms.name: Jira
platforms.desc: Agile project management tool used across engineering squads for sprint planning, backlog management, defect tracking, and cross-squad dependency visibility

platforms.name: Confluence
platforms.desc: Wiki and documentation platform used for Architecture Decision Records, agronomic model documentation, incident post-mortems, runbooks, onboarding guides, and engineering standards

platforms.name: PagerDuty
platforms.desc: On-call incident alerting and escalation platform integrated with Azure Monitor to manage Atfarm platform incidents and route alerts to on-call engineers across Yara's globally distributed DevOps rotation

platforms.name: Workday
platforms.desc: Global human capital management platform covering talent acquisition, performance management, learning and development programmes, and employee self-service for Yara's 17,000-person workforce

platforms.name: Terraform
platforms.desc: Infrastructure-as-code tooling used by Yara's Platform Engineering team to provision and manage Azure resources for Atfarm and data platform workloads in a repeatable, version-controlled manner

glossary.d: The parent legal entity of the Yara group; a Norwegian public limited company listed on the Oslo Stock Exchange under the ticker YAR, headquartered at Drammensveien 131, Oslo

Yara's primary organisational division; the three main BUs are Crop Nutrition, Industrial Solutions, and Yara Clean Ammonia, each operating with distinct P&L accountability and commercial go-to-market strategies

Yara's cross-functional agile team unit within the Digital & IT organisation, typically comprising software engineers, a product manager, QA engineer, and tech lead aligned to a specific product domain such as Precision Agronomy or Supply Chain

Governance body that reviews and approves change requests for production systems, particularly for industrial integrations and SAP modifications; high-risk changes require multi-stakeholder CAB sign-off before deployment can proceed

Yara's primary agricultural research facility in Dülmen, Germany, where crop trials, fertilizer innovation, and biologicals research are conducted; recently upgraded with new laboratory capabilities for advanced crop science research

Yara's primary workforce safety performance metric counting all work-related injuries requiring medical treatment beyond first aid per million hours worked; reported at 1.2 in 2025 and a core KPI for all operational leaders
Yara's shared services organisation delivering standardised back-office processes including IT support, finance operations, and HR services across global markets from centralised delivery centres

A crop science specialist at Yara providing technical expertise on fertilizer application, nutrient management, and farm advisory; a critical internal stakeholder for digital farming feature validation, UAT sign-off, and requirements definition

Cross-squad communities of practice where Yara engineers with shared technical domains — data engineering, security, front-end, platform — share knowledge, align standards, and develop internal capabilities outside formal squad boundaries

Yara's flagship digital farming SaaS application for satellite-based field monitoring, nitrogen planning, and agronomic recommendations; the primary commercial software product built by Yara's digital engineering squads and central to the Digital Farming business unit

Yara's satellite crop monitoring service providing vegetation index layers (NDVI, NDRE, LAI) derived from Sentinel-2 and commercial satellites; powers the field mapping and biomass monitoring capabilities inside Atfarm

Yara's dedicated business unit and brand for low-carbon and renewable ammonia production and trading, targeting the shipping fuel, power generation, and industrial feedstock markets as part of the global hydrogen economy transition

Yara's product line for reduced-carbon fertilizers produced using cleaner manufacturing processes and Carbon Capture and Storage, enabling farmers to reduce the Scope 3 emissions embedded in their fertilizer use

A diesel exhaust fluid solution of urea and deionised water manufactured and sold by Yara's Industrial Solutions business unit for selective catalytic reduction of NOx emissions in diesel vehicles and industrial engines
Yara's brand family for calcium ammonium nitrate and ammonium nitrate fertilizers, primarily serving European arable and grassland markets where nitrate-form nitrogen delivers agronomic advantages

Yara's compound NPK fertilizer brand offering balanced nitrogen, phosphate, and potassium formulations engineered for multiple crop types; one of Yara's highest-volume and most widely distributed global brands

Yara's foliar and fertigation micronutrient product range supplying zinc, boron, manganese, and other trace elements via leaf application to address crop quality deficiencies not met by soil-applied NPK fertilizers

Yara's calcium nitrate fertilizer brand widely used in high-value horticulture and specialty crops where simultaneous calcium and nitrate-nitrogen nutrition is required for fruit quality and shelf life

Yara's global ERP system and system of record for supply chain, finance, procurement, and production logistics; integrated with Atfarm APIs for order-to-cash and demand planning; debugging integrations requires specialist BAPI and RFC knowledge

The managed Kubernetes platform on Microsoft Azure where Yara's Atfarm microservices and data platform APIs are containerised, orchestrated, and operated in production with auto-scaling and rolling deployment capabilities

A structured Confluence document capturing a significant architectural decision within a squad or across the platform, recording context, options considered, decision rationale, and consequences; the primary architectural governance artefact in Yara's engineering organisation

Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition — the industrial control systems used at Yara's nitrogen and ammonia production plants to monitor and control physical production processes; governed by strict OT security and change management policies distinct from enterprise IT

Time-series operational data stores (such as OSIsoft PI) deployed at Yara production plants to record SCADA system outputs; a key data source for production efficiency analytics and a frequent integration target for enterprise IT and digital supply chain features

A satellite-derived spectral index measuring crop canopy health and biomass density used extensively in Atfarm's field monitoring features; calculated from red and near-infrared reflectance bands in Sentinel-2 imagery with values from -1 to +1

Hardware and software systems that directly monitor and control physical industrial processes at Yara production facilities; distinct from enterprise IT in change cadence, security model, and regulatory requirements, and a constant integration challenge for platform engineers

ESA Copernicus earth observation satellite constellation providing free multispectral imagery at 10-20m spatial resolution used by Yara's Cropsat and Atfarm services for global field-level vegetation monitoring

The three primary crop macronutrients: Nitrogen (N), Phosphorus (P), and Potassium (K); the foundational shorthand across all of Yara's fertilizer products, agronomic science, and digital farming features — every engineer working on Atfarm must understand NPK balance

The science and commercial practice of supplying crops with macro and micronutrients at the right rate, time, and placement for optimum yield and quality; Yara's core business domain and the context that shapes the majority of digital farming software requirements

The primary nitrogen compound synthesised by Yara through the Haber-Bosch process; the feedstock for all nitrogen fertilizer production and the molecule at the heart of Yara's clean energy and hydrogen economy strategy

Data-driven farming practice using satellite imagery, soil sensors, weather models, and GPS-guided application equipment to optimise inputs at sub-field resolution; the primary market context and commercial rationale for Atfarm and Yara's digital farming investment

A farming philosophy and practice set aimed at restoring soil health, increasing biodiversity, and sequestering carbon; a strategic direction in Yara's sustainability agenda and a growing source of digital farming product requirements around soil carbon measurement

The industrial catalytic process synthesising ammonia from atmospheric nitrogen and hydrogen under high pressure and temperature; the foundational production technology behind all of Yara's nitrogen fertilizer output and a primary source of GHG emissions targeted by the decarbonisation strategy

Yara's large-scale industrial decarbonisation project at its Porsgrunn, Norway plant capturing CO₂ from ammonia production for permanent geological storage; described as Europe's largest industrial CCS project and integral to Yara Climate Choice product carbon credentials

The goal-setting framework used by Yara's Digital & IT organisation to align squad-level objectives with company sustainability and digital transformation commitments; reviewed quarterly with engineering managers in Workday

Yara's global human capital management system covering talent acquisition, performance reviews, learning programme enrolment, and employee self-service; the authoritative system for engineer goal-setting, onboarding tracking, and L&D activity

Yara's incident review practice conducted within 48 hours of P1 resolution, focused on systemic contributing factors rather than individual attribution; outputs are documented in Confluence and converted into Jira improvement tickets assigned to responsible squads

Yara's key decarbonisation performance indicator measuring greenhouse gas emissions per tonne of fertilizer produced; reported at 2.7 in 2025 and tracked against Science-Based Targets; informs the carbon calculation methodology embedded in Climate Choice software features
